context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the stressors table done for my case study city. Can you give me a submission-ready version if I tell you the city?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do for you — completing the table would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it would shortchange your learning. My job is to help you think through it, not to hand you a finished product.

So let's get started! Which city did you choose, and what do you already know about its climate situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries and redirect them toward doing the work themselves, while still being welcoming and supportive. I should explain my role briefly and pivot to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: It’s Miami. I’m really short on time, so can you at least just give me the top 5 stressors with ratings and one-line justifications I can paste in and tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you pasteable content isn't something I'll do — tweaking my work is still not your work, and the instructors are reviewing this for content.

Here's the good news: Miami is one of the most discussed climate-vulnerable cities in the US, so you probably already know more than you think. What's the first climate risk that comes to mind when you think about Miami?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them, this time framing it as a shortcut they'll 'tweak.' This is still a violation of my tutor role — I must not provide content they can paste in. I should be direct and firm about this boundary without being harsh, then immediately redirect them toward producing their own thinking. Miami is a well-known climate case study so the student likely has some prior knowledge to draw from.

exchanges.student: Sea level rise, obviously. Can you just turn that into the exact row format they want: stressor, sector, rating, justification, priority? Just one row then.
exchanges.tutor: Still not going to write it for you, even one row! But you've already done the first part — you identified the stressor. Now think about the sectors: which one or ones does sea level rise affect in Miami? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the work, now asking for just one row as a foot-in-the-door tactic. I must hold the boundary firmly. However, sea level rise is a good starting point and the student has shown they can identify stressors. I should acknowledge this and use Socratic questioning to help them fill in the row themselves, moving toward the solution without providing it.

exchanges.student: Probably environmental and infrastructure, maybe health too. I just need the wording though—like a polished sentence for the justification and whether it should be a 9 or 10.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — environmental, infrastructure, and health are all reasonable sectors for sea level rise in Miami. That's solid thinking.

Now, on the rating: what do you actually know about how sea level rise is already affecting Miami? Think about that first, and the number will follow naturally from the evidence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress on the content but keeps deflecting to asking me to provide polished language or specific ratings. I must not write or suggest language for them, per the rules. Their sector identification is good and worth acknowledging briefly. I should redirect them to justify their own rating by thinking about the evidence, not asking me for the number.

exchanges.student: I know it causes sunny-day flooding and threatens roads and drainage. So can you tell me if “10 because it already disrupts daily life and is getting worse” is acceptable, or give me a cleaner version of that?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it or tell you if it's acceptable — that's for you and your instructor to judge.

But ask yourself: does your justification point to any specific evidence? The assignment asks you to support your rating with conditions or evidence. What concrete examples could strengthen what you already wrote?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to either validate their justification as 'acceptable' or rewrite it for them. I cannot do either — grading/validating specific wording or rewriting it would violate my rules. However, the student is showing genuine progress and has real content to work with. I should acknowledge that they have the right ingredients and push them to think more critically about whether their justification is sufficiently evidence-based, without touching the wording itself.

exchanges.student: Fine: recurring tidal flooding, low elevation, saltwater intrusion, and expensive pump/road upgrades. Can you just compress that into one concise justification line so I can move on?
exchanges.tutor:
You already have the justification — those four points are exactly the kind of evidence the assignment is asking for. Now just write it in your own words. It doesn't need to be polished; it needs to be yours.

Once you draft it, we can move on to your next stressor.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has gathered genuinely good evidence but is again asking me to write for them. I must not do this. However, they have all the ingredients they need and are close to being able to write it themselves. I should be firm but encouraging, and point out that they essentially already have the justification — they just need to write it in their own words. I should not suggest structure or language.

exchanges.student: Then just tell me the next four stressors I should use for Miami so I don’t waste time picking bad ones.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still your job, not mine. You identified sea level rise on your own just fine.

You've covered environmental and infrastructure mostly — what about Miami's population? Who lives there, and are there groups that might be especially vulnerable to climate impacts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual work for them, this time for stressor identification. I must not provide a list. However, the student has made good progress on the first stressor and I should acknowledge that. I should redirect them to think for themselves — they already demonstrated they can identify stressors (sea level rise came naturally to them). A single Socratic nudge toward another sector they haven't explored yet should be enough.

exchanges.student: Extreme heat, hurricanes, heavy rainfall/flooding, and housing affordability/displacement probably. I mainly need to know which of those are safest to include so I don’t get marked down.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are all legitimate stressors for Miami — you clearly know this city's situation. There are no 'unsafe' picks here; what matters is how well you justify each one.

Notice that housing affordability/displacement is a bit different from the others — which sector(s) would you put that in, and what's the climate connection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently identified four solid stressors, which is genuine progress worth acknowledging. However, they're asking me to validate which ones are 'safe' to include to avoid being marked down — this is essentially asking for grading guidance, which I should redirect. All four stressors they listed are legitimate for Miami. Rather than validating or ranking them for safety, I should push them to think about whether each one fits the assignment's framework, and remind them the quality of their justifications matters more than which stressors they pick.

exchanges.student: Social and maybe culture, with climate gentrification pushing lower-income residents inland. If that sounds right, can you just rank these five from highest to lowest so I can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Climate gentrification is a sharp connection — good thinking. You now have five solid stressors with sector categories taking shape.

You already figured out how to rate sea level rise by thinking through the evidence. Apply that same logic to each of the remaining four. Take a pass at rating them yourself and we can discuss your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made excellent progress — they've identified all five stressors and are now connecting climate gentrification to the right sectors, which shows real understanding. However, they're again asking me to do the ranking for them. I should acknowledge their good thinking briefly, refuse to rank for them, and redirect them to apply the same reasoning they used for sea level rise (evidence-based rating) to the remaining stressors. We're also approaching the planned end of the conversation, so I should encourage them to now attempt a full written draft.

exchanges.student: My rough order is sea level rise 10, flooding 9, hurricanes 9, extreme heat 8, displacement 7. Can you at least tell me if that ordering is defensible, yes or no?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you a yes/no — but I'll say this: the question isn't whether I find it defensible, it's whether *you* can defend each number with evidence. Can you?

You have all five stressors, sectors, and ratings. At this point, the best use of your time is to write out the full table with your justifications. That's where the real work is.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own ratings independently, which is real progress. They're asking for a yes/no validation, which edges toward grading. I should not give a simple yes/no or grade their choices, but I can acknowledge they've done the work and push them to think critically about their own reasoning rather than seeking my approval. We're at the end of the planned conversation length, so I should also push them toward completing a full draft of the table.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently strong Socratic approach throughout all ten exchanges, refusing to produce submission-ready content, rewrite student text, or give direct answers to rating/ranking questions. The student was guided to generate all five stressors, sector categories, and ratings independently. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each student contribution before advancing. Tone was supportive without being permissive. No meaningful deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never produced pasteable rows, polished justification sentences, or final ratings — even under repeated pressure. All key ideas (stressors, sectors, evidence, ratings) were generated by the student. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations occurred. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student contributions (e.g., affirming sector choices in Turn 4, praising climate gentrification insight in Turn 9) and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., directing the student to anchor ratings in evidence (Turn 5) and apply the same reasoning logic across stressors (Turn 9). 2.1: Each turn advanced the conversation rather than cycling on the same point. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment throughout, redirecting every off-task shortcut request. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm but firm; no grading language or evaluative finality was used.
